title: 02. LLM basics
tagline: Tokens, context windows, and sampling — see them, break them, then reason about cost.
description: What actually happens inside a model call: tokenize your own text, overflow a context window, and learn to pick models on the cost/quality/latency triangle.

exercises.title: Why everything is measured in tokens
exercises.content:
# Why everything is measured in tokens

Models don't see words or characters — they see **tokens**, learned chunks of text. `unbelievable` might split into `un`+`believ`+`able`; common words like `the` are a single token. In English, one token ≈ 4 characters ≈ ¾ of a word.

### Why you should care

**1. Cost.** APIs bill per token, input *and* output. A verbose system prompt sent on every request is a subscription you're paying forever. Trimming a 2,000-token prompt to 800 cuts that bill by 60% — before you touch anything else.

**2. Latency.** Output tokens are generated *one at a time* — remember the token predictor from course 01. A 2× longer answer takes roughly 2× longer to finish. Want a faster app? Ask for shorter answers (`max_tokens`, "answer in 2 sentences").

**3. Languages aren't equal.** Tokenizers are trained mostly on English. The same sentence in Chinese, Korean, or emoji can cost 2–3× more tokens — which means higher cost *and* less room in the context window.

**4. Weird failure modes.** Tokenization explains classic LLM stumbles: counting letters in a word ('how many r's in strawberry') is hard when the model never sees letters — only chunks.

### The mental math every builder does

- ~4 chars per token (English)
- price = (input tokens × input rate) + (output tokens × output rate)
- output rates are typically 3–5× input rates

In the next lesson you'll tokenize your own text and watch the counter — build the intuition now, save real money later.
exercises.attribution: Adapted from "Generative AI for Beginners" by Microsoft (MIT) and Maxime Labonne's LLM Course (Apache-2.0)

exercises.title: Playground: Tokenize your own text
exercises.content:
# Tokenize your own text

Type anything below — your name, a prompt you use at work, a sentence in another language, a wall of emoji — and watch it split into tokens with a live cost estimate.

Things worth trying:

- A long English word vs. several short ones
- The same sentence in English and another language
- Emoji (each one is often 2–3 tokens!)

*To complete this lesson: edit the text so it produces at least 20 tokens.*

exercises.title: Knowledge check: tokens
exercises.content:
# Knowledge check

Three questions on tokens. All three correct to pass — you've got this.

quiz.prompt: Your API bill doubled this month with the same number of users. Which change most likely caused it?
options: You switched the app's font to a wider one
You added a long system prompt that is sent with every single request
Users started asking questions in the evening
quiz.explanation: System prompts are input tokens on every call. Adding 1,500 tokens of instructions to every request is the classic silent bill-doubler.

quiz.prompt: Users complain the AI answer takes too long to finish. What's the most direct fix?
options: Constrain the output — shorter answers mean fewer tokens to generate
Move the server closer to the users
Increase the temperature so the model decides faster
quiz.explanation: Output tokens are generated sequentially, so answer length dominates generation time. Temperature changes *which* token is picked, not how fast.

quiz.prompt: Why can the same message cost more tokens in Chinese or emoji than in English?
options: The API charges a foreign-language fee
Tokenizers are trained mostly on English, so other scripts split into more, smaller pieces
Unicode characters are physically larger
quiz.explanation: Token vocabularies reflect training data. Text unlike that data — CJK scripts, emoji, rare symbols — fragments into more tokens per character.

exercises.title: Playground: Overflow a context window
exercises.content:
# The context window: the model's entire world

A model has no memory. Its **context window** — everything you send plus everything it has said — is *all it knows* during a call. When the conversation outgrows the window, something has to be thrown away, and the model won't tell you it happened.

Below is a conversation you control. Add messages, paste "documents", and watch the window fill. When it overflows, the oldest messages silently vanish — exactly what happens in production chatbots.

*To complete this lesson: overflow the window at least once. Then switch to the 200k window and notice how the same documents barely register.*

exercises.title: Video: Tokens & temperature in a real app
exercises.content:
### Watch: Building text generation applications

Microsoft's lesson on building your first text-generation app — the moment tokens and temperature stop being trivia and become knobs in your code. Watch for:

- Where `max_tokens` appears in a real API call and what happens when it's too small.
- How the presenters reason about **prompt cost**.
- The temperature values they actually pick for different tasks.
exercises.attribution: Video © Microsoft, "Generative AI for Beginners" — MIT license — github.com/microsoft/generative-ai-for-beginners

exercises.title: Sampling controls & the model-selection triangle
exercises.content:
# Sampling controls & the model-selection triangle

### The knobs on generation

You met **temperature** in course 01 — it reshapes the token distribution. Its siblings:

- **top-p (nucleus sampling)** — only sample from the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities add to *p*. `top_p: 0.9` cuts off the weird tail regardless of temperature.
- **max_tokens** — a hard stop on output length. Too small and answers get cut mid-sentence; too large and a runaway generation costs you money.
- **stop sequences** — strings that end generation early, great for keeping structured output clean.

Rule of thumb: tune **either** temperature or top-p, not both at once — you'll have no idea which knob did what.

### Inference vs. training

Everything above happens at **inference** — the model's weights never change. Your conversations don't teach it anything. **Fine-tuning** *does* change weights, but it teaches *behavior* (format, tone, task patterns), not fresh knowledge — that's what RAG is for (course 04).

### The cost / quality / latency triangle

Every model choice trades between three corners:

| Corner | Favors | Example task |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Small/budget models | Classify support tickets at 1M/day |
| **Quality** | Frontier models | Legal-document analysis, complex agents |
| **Latency** | Small models, short outputs | Autocomplete, live chat suggestions |

The pro move: **route by task**. Use a budget model for the 80% of easy requests and escalate the hard 20% to a frontier model. Teams that do this cut costs by 10× without users noticing.

The final two lessons make you prove it: a quiz on real trade-offs, then the code lab where you implement `recommendSettings` yourself.
exercises.attribution: Adapted from Maxime Labonne's LLM Course (Apache-2.0) and "Generative AI for Beginners" by Microsoft (MIT)

exercises.title: Knowledge check: picking the right setup
exercises.content:
# Knowledge check

Four scenario questions — the kind you'd get in a system-design interview. At least 3 to pass.

quiz.prompt: You're building autocomplete that fires on every keystroke. Which corner of the triangle dominates?
options: Quality — suggestions must be perfect
Latency — a suggestion that arrives late is worthless, so use a small fast model
Cost — pick the cheapest model regardless of speed
quiz.explanation: Autocomplete is worthless if it lags behind typing. Small, fast models with tiny outputs win here — quality just needs to be 'good enough to accept sometimes'.

quiz.prompt: You're extracting invoice numbers from emails into JSON. Which settings make sense?
options: Temperature ~0 with a stop sequence after the JSON — you want repeatable, parseable output
Temperature 1.2 so the model can be flexible about formats
Maximum max_tokens so nothing is ever cut off
quiz.explanation: Extraction is a determinism task: low temperature, constrained output. High temperature adds exactly the creativity you don't want in structured data.

quiz.prompt: The model doesn't know about products you launched last month. Will fine-tuning fix it?
options: Yes — fine-tuning is how you teach models new facts
It's the wrong tool — fine-tuning shapes behavior; fresh facts should come in via retrieval (RAG)
No model can ever use information newer than its training data
quiz.explanation: Fine-tuning bakes in patterns, not a knowledge feed — and you'd have to re-tune for every launch. Retrieval puts current facts into the context at question time.

quiz.prompt: Your AI feature costs too much at scale, but quality drops if you switch everything to a budget model. What's the standard play?
options: Route: budget model for easy requests, frontier model only for the hard ones
Cache one answer and serve it to everyone
Remove the feature — the triangle can't be beaten
quiz.explanation: Model routing is how real products beat the triangle: most traffic is easy and cheap to serve; only genuinely hard cases pay frontier prices.

exercises.title: Code lab: Choose generation settings
exercises.content:
# Choose generation settings

LLM output changes with context size, temperature, and the task type. Builders should pick settings intentionally instead of copying defaults — time to encode the rules you just learned.

### Your task
Implement `recommendSettings(task)` and return an object with:

- `temperature`: a number
- `reason`: a short string

Use low temperature for factual extraction and higher temperature for brainstorming.

### Source bench
- mlabonne LLM Course
- Microsoft Generative AI for Beginners
exercises.hint: The exact returned objects in the tests must match. Use task keyword checks like extract, brainstorm, and summarize.
